channel.title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - Power
channel.description: Curated OT/ICS advisories, incidents and threat intelligence. Editorial by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

item.title: [Critical] Federal alert sharpens: Iran-affiliated hackers are actively manipulating the PLCs that run US energy infrastructure
item.description: Federal agencies sharpened their warning that Iran-affiliated hackers are actively manipulating the programmable logic controllers that run US energy infrastructure. The joint advisory AA26-097A, updated 22 July, confirms actors have reached internet-exposed Rockwell, Schneider Electric and Siemens controllers, stolen project files, and rewritten or deleted control logic — in some cases disabling the shutdown and alarm functions that keep equipment inside safe limits, without alerting operators. For grid and generation operators the concern is direct: the same brands sit in substations, plants and remote sites, and a controller that suppresses its own alarms undermines the protection logic crews rely on. The campaign has already caused operational disruption and financial loss across the energy sector. It is not sophisticated — it targets devices left reachable from the internet with weak credentials. - CISA/FBI joint advisory AA26-097A - Updated 22 Jul 2026 (originally 7 Apr 2026) — ongoing campaign

item.title: [High] FSB Center 16 router campaign (AA26-194A): the exposed edge gear sitting in front of substation networks
item.description: A joint advisory from NSA, CISA, the FBI and roughly a dozen partner nations (AA26-194A, 13 July) details how Russia’s FSB Center 16 — tracked as Static Tundra, Berserk Bear, Energetic Bear and Dragonfly — scans for network devices exposing SNMP with default community strings and abuses Cisco Smart Install to copy device configurations. Energy and state/local government are named explicitly. The same internet-facing edge routers, often with SNMP left open, sit directly in front of substation SCADA and control-center networks, and a stolen config is a ready-made map of the network before an intruder touches a relay or RTU. - NSA/CISA/FBI joint advisory AA26-194A - Published 13 Jul 2026 — long-running campaign, ongoing

item.title: [Critical] FortiSandbox RCE flaws added to CISA KEV — exploited edge appliances on the utility IT/OT boundary
item.description: CISA added two critical Fortinet vulnerabilities, CVE-2026-39808 and CVE-2026-25089 (both CVSS 9.1), to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on 16 July after evidence of exploitation in the wild. The flaws are OS command-injection bugs in FortiSandbox, FortiSandbox Cloud and PaaS that let an unauthenticated attacker run commands via crafted HTTP requests. Fortinet appliances routinely sit on the security boundary between utility IT and OT; a compromised inspection appliance is both a foothold and a blind spot. CISA set a 19 July federal remediation deadline. - CVE-2026-39808 - Added to KEV 16 Jul 2026 — actively exploited

item.title: [High] Solar and storage inverters flagged as a remote-sabotage path as the grid leans on DER
item.description: Renewed research and industry attention this month centers on the cyber exposure of grid-connected solar inverters and battery storage as distributed energy resources become a larger share of generation. Analysts warn that inverters and their cloud-management platforms can be remotely manipulated — plaintext control channels, predictable session handling and internet-exposed management have all surfaced in recent DER advisories — and that coordinated manipulation of enough inverter capacity could stress grid stability. For utilities integrating third-party DER, the takeaway is that inverter fleets are OT assets that need the same segmentation, monitoring and vendor-security scrutiny as any control system. - SEIA / Cybersecurity Dive / CISA - Jul 2026 — ongoing DER exposure research

item.title: [Critical] Schneider Easergy MiCOM P40 protection relays: unauthenticated SNMP RCE could disrupt substation protection
item.description: CISA published ICSA-26-190-03 (Schneider SEVD-2026-104-03) on 9 July, warning of a critical remote-code-execution flaw, CVE-2026-4832, in Schneider Electric’s Easergy MiCOM P40 protection relays. The bug is a memory-safety error in the relay’s SNMP implementation: an unauthenticated attacker sending crafted SNMP packets to an unpatched device could disrupt operation of a relay that sits at the heart of substation protection. No public exploitation is known, but protection relays are high-consequence assets, so this is a patch-and-monitor priority rather than an emergency. - CVE-2026-4832 - Published 9 Jul 2026

item.title: [High] Hitachi Energy PROMOD V and e-mesh EMS advisories add to the July grid-vendor patch load
item.description: On 7 July CISA published a batch of ICS advisories including flaws in Hitachi Energy PROMOD V and the e-mesh energy management system. These are planning and energy-management platforms used by utilities rather than field controllers, but they hold sensitive operational data and sit inside utility environments, so unpatched instances widen the attack surface. No exploitation is reported; the action is routine but real — track affected versions and schedule the vendor fixes. - Hitachi Energy PSIRT / CISA / Industrial Cyber - Published 7 Jul 2026

item.title: [Info] FERC-approved CIP virtualization standards and INSM monitoring set the grid’s compliance runway
item.description: FERC Order 919 approved an updated set of CIP standards (CIP-002-7 through CIP-013-3) covering virtualized environments, effective 26 May 2026 with mandatory compliance by 1 July 2028. Separately, CIP-015-1 (Order 907) requires Internal Network Security Monitoring for applicable control centers by 2 September 2028 and other entities by 2 September 2030. Nothing changes operationally today, but planning for INSM and virtualization compliance — visibility inside the electronic security perimeter — is work that starts now. - FERC Orders 919 & 907 / NERC / Industrial Cyber - Effective 26 May 2026; compliance 1 Jul 2028 (INSM Sep 2028)

item.title: [Info] SEIA publishes cybersecurity priorities for the US solar and storage industry
item.description: The Solar Energy Industries Association released a report this month, “Cybersecurity Priorities for America’s Solar & Storage Industry,” setting out measures to harden inverter technology — supply-chain traceability, national inverter-cybersecurity guidance, and shared resources across the industry. It is guidance, not a mandate, but it signals where DER security expectations are heading and gives utilities and developers a reference as inverter fleets grow. - SEIA / Solar Power World - Published Jul 2026
